context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just draft 2 full components for my city case study so i can submit smth tn?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing your assignment for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't actually help you learn the material either.

But I can absolutely help you work through it right now. Let's start simple: which city did you choose for your case study, and what do you already know about its main climate vulnerabilities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to clearly decline and redirect them to engage with the work themselves. I should be direct but encouraging, and pivot quickly to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: it’s mexico city, and tbh i already know the basics—water stress, heat, air pollution. can u just turn that into 2 polished components fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — the writing has to come from you.

But you're actually in a decent starting position. Water stress, heat, and air pollution are all real pressure points for Mexico City. Which two do you want to build your components around? And do you have a sense of which communities in the city are most affected by each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. However, they've given me useful starting material (Mexico City, water stress, heat, air pollution) that I can work with Socratically. I should acknowledge their solid starting point and redirect them toward choosing which two vulnerabilities to focus on, which is a productive next step that they need to decide themselves.

exchanges.student: prob water + heat, but i'm rly short on time rn. can u at least give me submission-ready outlines w topic sentences so i can paste em in?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but outlines with topic sentences is still me writing your assignment — I won't do that regardless of the deadline.

Here's what we can do right now: pick one component — water stress or heat — and tell me in your own words what the current situation is in Mexico City. Even two rough sentences. That's your Context section starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to write content for them, now framing it as 'outlines with topic sentences.' This is still doing the writing for them, which I must refuse clearly. Time pressure is not a valid reason to bypass the learning process. I should be firm but redirect constructively toward the smallest productive action they can take right now.

exchanges.student: ok fine: mexico city has unequal water access and overdrawn aquifers, plus flooding when storms hit. can u rewrite that better but keep it sounding academic?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rewrite it — that's your job.

But honestly, what you wrote isn't bad as a rough start. Now push it further: *why* are the aquifers overdrawn, and who in the city suffers most when water access is unequal? That thinking will naturally sharpen your writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to edit and polish their writing, which the rules explicitly forbid. I must decline clearly. However, what they wrote is actually a reasonable start for the Context section, and I should acknowledge that and push them to develop it further themselves rather than rewriting it.

exchanges.student: idk, peripheral low-income areas get hit hardest and infrastructure leaks a lot. just give me 2-3 cleaner sentences i can build from, not the whole thing.
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — I won't write sentences for you, even short ones.

But look at what you already have: overdrawn aquifers, leaky infrastructure, unequal access hitting low-income peripheral areas. That's solid context. Try putting those three ideas into your own sentences right now. What do you think should come first logically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to write sentences for them, just framed as 'a few cleaner sentences.' I must refuse this too, as the rules explicitly state I should not write or suggest language. However, they are actually building good content knowledge — peripheral low-income areas and infrastructure leaks are relevant and accurate. I should affirm the substance and redirect them to put it together themselves, asking a focused question to help them connect their ideas into a coherent context statement.

exchanges.student: then just gimme a fill-in-the-blank template for the water component fr.
exchanges.tutor:
No templates either — that's still me doing the writing.

Look, you've been trying to find a workaround for a few messages now, and you actually already have everything you need: aquifer depletion, leaky infrastructure, low-income peripheral communities bearing the burden. Just write a rough paragraph. It doesn't need to be perfect — that's why it's a *draft*. What's stopping you from just putting those ideas down in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying different angles to get me to do the writing for them. A fill-in-the-blank template would essentially be writing the component structure for them. I need to firmly decline and note that they've been going in circles. It's time to be direct about this pattern and push them to just write something — they clearly have enough knowledge to do so.

exchanges.student: honestly i just need smth fast lol. what policy name would sound legit for a water component on mexico city?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still yours to come up with.

Think about it this way: what do you actually want the policy to *do* — fix the leaky infrastructure, regulate water use, expand access to underserved areas? The name should reflect the intervention. What's your policy actually targeting?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to contribute to their writing, this time by asking for a policy name. Even this small ask is part of the assignment they need to do themselves. However, I can redirect them productively by asking them what kind of intervention they're actually proposing — that will naturally lead them to name it themselves.

exchanges.student: like reducing leaks + improving access in underserved areas. can u at least list 5 possible policy names?
exchanges.tutor:
You literally just described your policy in one sentence — reducing leaks and improving access in underserved areas. That's your name, just condense it.

What's a short phrase that captures both of those goals?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to generate content for them, now asking for a list of policy names. I must refuse. However, they've just articulated their policy focus quite clearly — reducing leaks and improving access in underserved areas. That's enough for them to name it themselves. I should point this back at them directly.

exchanges.student: ok then is “Equitable Water Infrastructure Retrofit Program” too generic?
exchanges.tutor:
That works — it clearly communicates the focus. Don't overthink the name.

You now have your Component 1 policy name and you know your context points. Take a shot at writing the Context section in 2-3 sentences. What's the current situation and key challenge in Mexico City?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is genuine progress — the student came up with a policy name on their own. I should acknowledge this positively without over-praising. Rather than dwelling on the name (which doesn't need to be perfect at draft stage), I should push them forward to actually start writing the Context section, since we've been circling around that for a while.

exchanges.student: yeah prob. for component 2, can u just tell me if “Neighborhood Heat Resilience and Cooling Access Initiative” sounds ok?
exchanges.tutor:
That's fine too. You now have names for both components.

But let's be real — you've spent several messages on naming and framing and haven't written a single sentence of actual content yet. Your draft is due soon. Start writing your Component 1 Context section right now and paste it here — we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student came up with another reasonable policy name on their own, which is good progress. However, they've been stuck on naming and framing for a while without producing any actual written content. I should briefly acknowledge the name is fine and push them firmly toward actually writing something — they have both policy names now and enough context knowledge. It's time to redirect to the bigger picture and get them writing.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, when the student asks for a fill-in-the-blank template, the tutor jumps straight to addressing the request and reiterating what the student already has, rather than asking a diagnostic question to understand what specifically is blocking the student from writing. Similarly in Turn 5, the tutor repeats the same encouragement without probing why the student is stuck. However, this is a borderline case since the tutor does ask 'What's stopping you?' in Turn 6, which partially functions as a diagnostic question.

The tutor provides encouragement ('that's solid context,' 'that works') but rarely gives specific method-level feedback on how the student should approach structuring their reasoning, connecting evidence to policy, or thinking about EJ implications. Feedback tends to be validating or redirecting rather than process-instructive.

The tutor cycles through the same refusal-and-redirect pattern across Turns 1–8 without meaningfully advancing the student's understanding or switching to a different approach. The student remains stuck and keeps asking for workarounds; the tutor repeats the same move (refuse, list what student already has, ask them to write) without reframing, simplifying, or trying a different scaffolding strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently and correctly refuses to do the student's work, maintaining academic integrity throughout. Responses are concise and appropriately toned. The main weaknesses are: (1) a repetitive refusal-and-redirect loop that fails to break the student's impasse through alternative scaffolding strategies, and (2) limited meta-learning feedback — the tutor validates student ideas but rarely explains the reasoning process or methodology the student should be developing. The 1.2.B.a deduction is borderline since Turn 6 does include a diagnostic question, but the pattern across multiple turns shows insufficient probing before repeating the same approach.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never writes assignment content, rewrites student text, or gives submission-ready material. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because across multiple turns where the student is clearly stuck, the tutor defaults to restating what the student already has and asking them to write, rather than first diagnosing the specific barrier (e.g., conceptual confusion vs. writing anxiety vs. time management). 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because feedback like 'that's solid context' and 'that works' is validating but not process-instructive — the tutor never explains how to connect evidence to policy recommendations, how to think about EJ implications structurally, or how to approach the Interactions section. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor repeats the same refuse-list-redirect pattern across 8 turns without switching to reframing, targeted simplification, or a different scaffolding approach, leaving the student no closer to actually writing. 2.2 and section 3 receive full marks.
